what: Example 2: More advanced function of basic statistics
title: Lecture 1: An Introduction to R
text: function parameter () default value (F). means can call function without setting parameter. examples two alternatives:
code: basic.stats <- function(x, more=F) {   stats <- list()   clean.x <- x[!is.na(x)]   stats$n <- length(x)   stats$nNAs <- stats$n-length(clean.x)   stats$mean <- mean(clean.x)   stats$std <- sd(clean.x)   stats$med <- median(clean.x)   if (more) {     stats$skew <- sum(((clean.x - stats$mean)/stats$std)^3)/length(clean.x)     stats$kurt <- sum(((clean.x - stats$mean)/stats$std)^4)/length(clean.x)-3     }   unlist(stats)  } basic.stats(test) #>         n      nNAs      mean       std       med  #> 10.000000  0.000000  5.500000  2.877113  5.000000 basic.stats(test,more=T) #>          n       nNAs       mean        std        med       skew       kurt  #> 10.0000000  0.0000000  5.5000000  2.8771128  5.0000000  0.5542469 -1.0902009

title: Lecture 1: An Introduction to R
text: Let’s create vector elements [1:5] belong group elements [6:10] belong group B Now let’s create permutation test: First calculate observed difference group averages test 500 permutations Draw histogram sampled differences  Let’s calculate p-value observed difference: Note p-value indicates probability getting observed difference averages random samples, abs() calculate two-sided p-value. Let’s calculate 95% confidence intervals p-value (approximation normal distribution):
code: y2=c(9.65, 5.09, 8.80, 7.42, 6.68, 8.79, 9.36, 9.64, 9.02, 8.86) obs.diff.means=mean(y2[1:5])-mean(y2[6:10]) diff.means=numeric() for (i in 1:500){  perm=sample(y2,10,replace=F)  diff.means[i]=mean(perm[1:5])-mean(perm[6:10])  } hist(diff.means) p2<-sum(abs(diff.means)>=abs(obs.diff.means))/500 p2 #> [1] 0.124 pm.part=1.96*sqrt(p2*(1-p2)/500) ucb=p2+pm.part lcb=p2-pm.part lcb;ucb #> [1] 0.0951109 #> [1] 0.1528891

title: Lecture 2: Reading data
text: know dataset want, construct WFS request URL retrieves 5 km statistical grid (called hila5km) GeoJSON format. st_read() function sf used download immediately load spatial data R sf object. , let´s construct WFS request load data directly sf object.
code: url_grid <- list(   hostname = "geo.stat.fi/geoserver/tilastointialueet/wfs",   scheme   = "https",   query = list(     service = "WFS",     version = "2.0.0",     request = "GetFeature",     typename = "tilastointialueet:hila5km",     outputFormat = "json"   ) ) %>% setattr("class", "url")  grid_url <- build_url(url_grid)  grid_5km <- st_read(grid_url) #> Reading layer `OGRGeoJSON' from data source  #>   `https://geo.stat.fi/geoserver/tilastointialueet/wfs/?service=WFS&version=2.0.0&request=GetFeature&typename=tilastointialueet%3Ahila5km&outputFormat=json'  #>   using driver `GeoJSON' #> Simple feature collection with 16113 features and 4 fields #> Geometry type: POLYGON #> Dimension:     XY #> Bounding box:  xmin: 60000 ymin: 6605000 xmax: 735000 ymax: 7780000 #> Projected CRS: ETRS89 / TM35FIN(E,N)

title: Lecture 2: Reading data
text: step, create simple map downloaded 5 km grid. ggplot2 geom_sf() functions allow us quickly inspect geometry structure grid, ensuring data loaded correctly looks expected.  Let’s break line line. ggplot() initializes new ggplot object. grid_5km sf object, meaning contains geometry (polygons) plus data. ’s sf object, ggplot automatically knows interpret geometry mapping. words: sets plotting environment using 5 km grid dataset Statistics Finland. key line draws map. geom_sf() special geom used plotting spatial features (points, lines, polygons) sf objects. aes(fill = id) Fills polygon using variable id. means every 5 km grid cell gets color based id value. id categorical, get distinct colors; numeric, gradient. color = NA Removes polygon borders. Without borders, grid cells blend smooth map, often visually cleaner. Adds title plot. Makes map clearer ready reporting presentations.
code: ggplot(grid_5km) +   geom_sf(aes(fill = id), color = NA) +   labs(title = "Statistics Finland 5 km Grid") 1. ggplot(grid_5km) 2. geom_sf(aes(fill = id), color = NA) 3. labs(title = "Statistics Finland 5 km Grid")

what: 8. Define Custom Colors for the Map and Create the Map
title: Lecture 2: Reading data
text: colors represent different municipal groups. visualize municipal groups using ggplot2 geom_sf(). aes(fill = kuntaryhma) fills polygons municipal group scale_fill_manual() applies custom color palette theme() adjusts legend position appearance
code: ccities<-"#FED789" #cities crural<-"#023743" #rural cdense<-"#72874E" #densely populated ggplot(municipalities2) +   geom_sf(aes(fill=kuntaryhma),           alpha=0.75,colour="white",lwd=0.1) +   scale_fill_manual(values = c(ccities, crural, cdense), name = "", guide = guide_legend(direction = "horizontal", label.position = "top", keywidth = 3, keyheight = 0.5)) +   theme(legend.position = c(0.16,0.7)) +   theme(legend.title=element_text(size=12),legend.text=element_text(size=12)) +   guides(fill=guide_legend(title="", nrow=3))

title: Lecture 2: Reading data
text: Start ggplot load data Starts new ggplot. data source municipalities2, sf (simple features) object containing polygons. following layers inherit dataset unless overridden. Draw municipality polygons : geom_sf() draws geometries inside municipalities2. aes(fill = kuntaryhma) maps polygon color variable kuntaryhma. alpha = 0.75 makes polygons slightly transparent. colour = “white” draws thin white borders around municipalities. lwd = 0.1 sets thin border line width. Set manual colors groups : - Assigns custom colors fill scale. - ccities, crural, cdense likely predefined color vectors. - name = “” removes legend title. legend guide customized: direction = “horizontal” → legend items horizontal line. label.position = “top” → labels appear color boxes. keywidth = 3 → wide legend keys. keyheight = 0.5 → flat, low legend keys. Move legend inside plot Places legend coordinates (0.16, 0.7) within plot area. Style legend text Increases legend text size readability. Even though legend title empty, code keeps styling consistent. Control number legend rows Fine‑tunes legend fill aesthetic. nrow = 3 arranges legend items three rows. title = “” ensures legend title appears.
code: ggplot(municipalities2) + geom_sf(aes(fill = kuntaryhma),         alpha = 0.75, colour = "white", lwd = 0.1) + scale_fill_manual(   values = c(ccities, crural, cdense),   name = "",   guide = guide_legend(     direction = "horizontal",     label.position = "top",     keywidth = 3,     keyheight = 0.5   ) ) + theme(legend.position = c(0.16, 0.7)) + theme(   legend.title = element_text(size = 12),   legend.text  = element_text(size = 12) ) + guides(fill = guide_legend(title = "", nrow = 3))

what: Example: Add polygons or shapefiles (e.g., municipal borders)
title: Lecture 2: Reading data
text: Using geofi::get_municipalities(), retrieve 2025 municipal borders sf object. keep municipality ID name. Municipalities object Finnish national grid ETRS89 / TM35FIN (EPSG:3067). Leaflet, however, needs WGS84 (EPSG:4326) coordinates (lat/lon). Transform municipalities WGS84: , can use
code: municipalities <- geofi::get_municipalities(year = 2024) #> Requesting response from: https://geo.stat.fi/geoserver/wfs?service=WFS&version=1.0.0&request=getFeature&typename=tilastointialueet%3Akunta4500k_2024 #> Warning: Coercing CRS to epsg:3067 (ETRS89 / TM35FIN) #> Data is licensed under: Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0) municipalities <- municipalities %>%    select(kunta, kunta_name) library(sf) muni_wgs84 <- st_transform(municipalities, crs = 4326) library(leaflet)  leaflet() %>%   addTiles() %>%   addPolygons(data = muni_wgs84, fillOpacity = 0.2, color = "blue")

text: Leaflet R allows attach HTML content popup, including embedded YouTube videos. example, create iframe snippet containing YouTube embed link, assign popup map marker. marker clicked, video plays directly inside map interface. can also add different backgrounds map following example. First, create basic Leaflet map store object m. map includes default OpenStreetMap tiles marker YouTube popup. Next, enhance map adding additional background layers. add Esri World Imagery satellite map 50% opacity allow blending, add CartoDB Voyager Labels layer display clear labels top satellite imagery. start m continue piping, layers added top original map without recreating . Typical Use Cases Visualizing geographic data (points, lines, polygons) Creating interactive dashboards (e.g., Shiny) Exploring geospatial datasets Teaching demonstrating spatial concepts
code: library(leaflet) youtube_iframe <- '<iframe width="300" height="200" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/JCizmc4tRxY" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen></iframe>' leaflet() %>%    addTiles() %>%    addMarkers(     lng = 26.9459, lat = 60.4666,   # Example coords (Helsinki)     popup = youtube_iframe   ) m<-leaflet() %>%    addTiles() %>%    addMarkers(     lng = 26.9459, lat = 60.4666,   # Example coords (Helsinki)     popup = youtube_iframe   ) m %>%   addProviderTiles(     providers$Esri.WorldImagery,     options = providerTileOptions(opacity = 0.5)   ) %>%   addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.VoyagerOnlyLabels)

title: Lecture 2: Reading data
text: Let’s continue previous example mapped municipality groups. also create interactive map dataset using leaflet package. creating interactive leaflet map, must ensure spatial data uses correct coordinate reference system (CRS). Leaflet requires data WGS84 geographic coordinates (EPSG:4326), use longitude latitude degrees. However, municipalities2 dataset currently projected CRS: TM35FIN system represents coordinates meters (UTM Zone 35), excellent Finnish spatial analysis, compatible leaflet, can display long‑lat coordinates. fix , use st_transform() function. st_transform() sf function reprojects spatial data new coordinate system. case, converts municipal polygons TM35FIN WGS84 (EPSG:4326), making usable leaflet. transformed, load leaflet library create interactive map. color palette different municipality groups reused ggplot example.
code:
Geometry type: MULTIPOLYGON Bounding box: xmin: 83747.59 ymin: 6637032 xmax: 732907.7 ymax: 7776431 Projected CRS: ETRS89 / TM35FIN(E,N) municipalities3 <- sf::st_transform(municipalities2, 4326) library(leaflet) # Define a color palette function based on kuntaryhma values pal <- colorFactor( palette = c(ccities, crural, cdense), domain = municipalities2$kuntaryhma ) leaflet_map <- leaflet(municipalities3) %>% addProviderTiles("CartoDB.Positron") %>% addPolygons( fillColor = ~pal(kuntaryhma), fillOpacity = 0.8, color = "white", weight = 1, popup = ~paste0( "", kunta_name, "
", "Group: ", kuntaryhma ), highlight = highlightOptions( weight = 2, color = "#444444", fillOpacity = 0.9, bringToFront = TRUE ) ) %>% addLegend( pal = pal, values = ~kuntaryhma, title = "Municipality Group", opacity = 1 ) leaflet_map

title: Lecture 2: Reading data
text: Start leaflet map data leaflet(municipalities3) initializes leaflet map. municipalities3 sf object. means leaflet automatically knows row geometry (polygon), lets refer attribute columns using formulas like ~kuntaryhma. Add background tile map Loads tile layer CartoDB Positron style (light, clean basemap). becomes background polygons drawn. Add municipal polygons core mapping step. Let’s break : 3.1 Color municipality using palette pal color palette function created earlier (e.g., colorFactor). ~kuntaryhma means: use kuntaryhma column municipalities3. municipality gets color matching group. 3.2 Set polygon styling fillOpacity: transparent fill color . color: border line color (white). weight: border thickness (1 pixel). 3.3 Add popup municipality Uses values data: kunta_name (municipality name), kuntaryhma (municipality group) paste0() builds HTML-formatted text popup. 3.4 Add interactive highlight mouse hover user hovers polygon: - Border becomes thicker (weight = 2) - Border turns dark gray (#444444) - Fill becomes less transparent (fillOpacity = 0.9) - Polygon brought front isn’t hidden Add legend explain colors Uses palette function (pal) used polygon colors. values = ~kuntaryhma tells legend categories appear. title sets legend title. opacity = 1 makes fully visible. steps, get interactive map : - Municipalities colored group - highlighting improves interactivity - Clicking shows detailed popups - Legend explains meaning colors - Clean basemap makes shapes look sharp
code:
leaflet_map <- leaflet(municipalities3) %>% addProviderTiles("CartoDB.Positron") %>% addPolygons( fillColor = ~pal(kuntaryhma), fillOpacity = 0.8, color = "white", weight = 1, popup = ~paste0( "", kunta_name, "
", "Group: ", kuntaryhma ), highlight = highlightOptions( weight = 2, color = "#444444", fillOpacity = 0.9, bringToFront = TRUE ) ) %>% fillColor = ~pal(kuntaryhma) fillOpacity = 0.8 color = "white" weight = 1 popup = ~paste0( "", kunta_name, "
", "Group: ", kuntaryhma ) highlight = highlightOptions( weight = 2, color = "#444444", fillOpacity = 0.9, bringToFront = TRUE ) addLegend( pal = pal, values = ~kuntaryhma, title = "Municipality Group", opacity = 1 )

title: Lecture 3: ggplot2
text: ggplot2 widely used data visualization package R lets create clear, powerful, customizable plots using consistent logical system called Grammar Graphics. details: https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/ ggplot2 popular: Declarative readable Easy extend modify Produces publication-quality figures Works perfectly tidy data Excellent support statistical graphics ggplot2 also official extension mechanism means others can now easily create stats, geoms positions, provide packages. can find information : https://exts.ggplot2.tidyverse.org/ggiraph.html Please, take look also webpage: https://github.com/erikgahner/awesome-ggplot2

title: Lecture 3: ggplot2
text: Instead choosing plot type (like “bar plot” “scatter plot”) directly, ggplot2 builds plots combining components (“layers”): Data – want plot Aesthetics (aes) – variables map visuals (x, y, color, size, …) Geoms – geometric objects draw (points, lines, bars, …) Scales – values map colors, sizes, axes Facets – splitting plots panels Themes – visual appearance (fonts, background, gridlines) add components together using +.

title: Lecture 3: ggplot2
text: Every ggplot2 plot three key components: data, set aesthetic mappings variables data visual properties, least one layer describes render observation. Layers usually created geom function.  produces scatterplot defined : Data - mtcars Aesthetic mapping: engine size mapped x position (wt), fuel economy y position (mpg). Layer - Geom: points (scatter plot) Pay attention structure function call: data aesthetic mappings supplied ggplot(), layers added +. important pattern, learn ggplot2 ’ll construct increasingly sophisticated plots adding types components. Almost every plot maps variable x y, naming aesthetics tedious, first two unnamed arguments aes() mapped x y. means following code identical example :
code: library(ggplot2) ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +   geom_point() ggplot(mpg, aes(displ, hwy)) +   geom_point()

title: Lecture 3: ggplot2
text: extend plot : adding regression line adding labels Plot geoms geom_smooth() fits smoother data displays smooth standard error. geom_boxplot() produces box--whisker plot summarise distribution set points. geom_histogram() geom_freqpoly() show distribution continuous variables. geom_bar() shows distribution categorical variables. geom_path() geom_line() draw lines data points. line plot constrained produce lines travel left right, paths can go direction. Lines typically used explore things change time. Note different geoms can use data aesthetics! Multiple geoms can layered like example:  overlays scatterplot smooth curve, including assessment uncertainty form point-wise confidence intervals shown grey. ’re interested confidence interval, turn geom_smooth(se = FALSE). important argument geom_smooth() method, allows choose type model used fit smooth curve: method = “loess”, default small n, uses smooth local regression (described ?loess). wiggliness line controlled span parameter, ranges 0 (exceedingly wiggly) 1 (wiggly).   method = “gam” fits generalised additive model provided mgcv package. need first load mgcv, use formula like formula = y ~ s(x) y ~ s(x, bs = “cs”) (large data). ggplot2 uses 1,000 points.
code: ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +   geom_point() +   geom_smooth(method = "lm") +   labs(     title = "Fuel efficiency vs car weight",     x = "Weight",     y = "Miles per gallon") #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' ggplot(mpg, aes(displ, hwy)) +    geom_point() +    geom_smooth() #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x' #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x' ggplot(mpg, aes(displ, hwy)) +    geom_point() +    geom_smooth(span = 0.2) #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x' #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x' ggplot(mpg, aes(displ, hwy)) +    geom_point() +    geom_smooth(span = 1) #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x' #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x' library(mgcv) #> Loading required package: nlme #>  #> Attaching package: 'nlme' #> The following object is masked from 'package:dplyr': #>  #>     collapse #> This is mgcv 1.9-4. For overview type '?mgcv'. ggplot(mpg, aes(displ, hwy)) +    geom_point() +    geom_smooth(method = "gam", formula = y ~ s(x))

title: Lecture 3: ggplot2
text: set data includes categorical variable one continuous variables, probably interested know values continuous variables vary levels categorical variable. three useful techniques help visualise categorical variable one continuous variable: Jittering, geom_jitter(), adds little random noise data can help avoid overplotting. Boxplots, geom_boxplot(), summarise shape distribution handful summary statistics. Violin plots, geom_violin(), show compact representation “density” distribution, highlighting areas points found. illustrated :
code: ggplot(mpg, aes(drv, hwy)) + geom_jitter() ggplot(mpg, aes(drv, hwy)) + geom_boxplot() ggplot(mpg, aes(drv, hwy)) + geom_violin()

title: Lecture 3: ggplot2
text: Line path plots typically used time series data. Line plots join points left right, path plots join order appear dataset (words, line plot path plot data sorted x value). Line plots usually time x-axis, showing single variable changed time. Path plots show two variables simultaneously changed time, time encoded way observations connected. year variable mpg dataset two values, ’ll show time series plots using economics dataset, contains economic data US measured last 40 years. figures shows two plots unemployment time, produced using geom_line(). first shows unemployment rate second shows median number weeks unemployed. can already see differences two variables, particularly last peak, unemployment percentage lower preceding peaks, length unemployment high.
code: ggplot(economics, aes(date, unemploy / pop)) +   geom_line() ggplot(economics, aes(date, uempmed)) +   geom_line()

what: How it differs from base R plots

text: Base R plotting Procedural “Draw now, modify later” Plot type chosen first (plot, hist, boxplot) ggplot2 Declarative “Describe plot” Visualization built components Base R:  ggplot2:  ggplot2 : structured consistent easier build complex plots incrementally See information ggplot2 fantastic book: “ggplot2: elegant graphics data analysis” https://ggplot2-book.org/

title: Lecture 3: ggplot2
text: Recently, package ggplot2 allowed use simple features package sf layers graph. combination ggplot2 sf therefore enables programmatically create maps, using grammar graphics, just informative visually appealing traditional GIS software. Let´s start loading basic packages necessary maps, .e. ggplot2 sf. package rnaturalearth provides map countries entire world. Use ne_countries pull country data choose scale (rnaturalearthhires necessary scale = “large”). function can return sp classes (default) directly sf classes, defined argument return class:
code: library("ggplot2") library("sf") #> Linking to GEOS 3.12.1, GDAL 3.8.4, PROJ 9.4.0; sf_use_s2() is TRUE library("rnaturalearth") library("rnaturalearthdata") #>  #> Attaching package: 'rnaturalearthdata' #> The following object is masked from 'package:rnaturalearth': #>  #>     countries110  world <- ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf") class(world) #> [1] "sf"         "data.frame"

title: Lecture 3: ggplot2
text: First, let us start creating base map world using ggplot2. base map extended different map elements, well zoomed area interest. can check world map properly retrieved converted sf object, plot ggplot2:  call nicely introduces structure ggplot call: first part ggplot(data = world) initiates ggplot graph, indicates main data stored world object. line ends + sign, indicates call complete yet, subsequent line correspond another layer scale. case, use geom_sf function, simply adds geometry stored sf object.
code: ggplot(data = world) +     geom_sf()

title: Lecture 3: ggplot2
text: title subtitle can added map using function ggtitle, passing valid character string (e.g. quotation marks) arguments. Axis names absent default map, can changed something suitable (e.g. “Longitude” “Latitude”), depending map:
code: ggplot(data = world) +     geom_sf() +     xlab("Longitude") + ylab("Latitude") +     ggtitle("World map", subtitle = paste0("(", length(unique(world$NAME)), " countries)"))

title: Lecture 3: ggplot2
text: many ways, sf geometries different regular geometries, can displayed level control attributes. example polygons countries filled green color (argument fill), using black outline countries (argument color):  package ggplot2 allows use complex color schemes, gradient one variable data. another example shows population country. example, use “viridis” colorblind-friendly palette color gradient (option = “plasma” plasma variant), using square root population (stored variable POP_EST world object):
code: ggplot(data = world) +      geom_sf(color = "black", fill = "lightgreen") ggplot(data = world) +     geom_sf(aes(fill = pop_est)) +     scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma", trans = "sqrt")

title: Lecture 3: ggplot2
text: function coord_sf allows deal coordinate system, includes projection extent map. default, map use coordinate system first layer defines one (.e. scanned order provided), none, fall back WGS84 (latitude/longitude, reference system used GPS). Using argument crs, possible override setting, project fly projection. can achieved using valid PROJ4 string (, European-centric ETRS89 Lambert Azimuthal Equal-Area projection):
code: ggplot(data = world) +     geom_sf() +     coord_sf(crs = "+proj=laea +lat_0=52 +lon_0=10 +x_0=4321000 +y_0=3210000 +ellps=GRS80 +units=m +no_defs ")

title: Lecture 3: ggplot2
text: Scale bar north arrow can added map package ggspatial, provides easy--use functions purpose.  lines add scale bar: Arguments explained: location = “bl” bottom-left corner map options include “bl”, “br”, “tl”, “tr” width_hint = 0.5 scale bar take half width plot hint, exact measurement lines add north arrow: Key parameters: Position: location = “bl” - bottom-left pad_x, pad_y - spacing plot edges measured inches prevents overlap scale bar Direction: which_north = “true” arrow points true north Style: style = north_arrow_fancy_orienteering decorative, compass-style arrow ggspatial provides several built-styles part code: Limits visible map area Uses longitude (xlim) latitude (ylim) change data , see
code: library("ggspatial") ggplot(data = world) +     geom_sf() +     annotation_scale(location = "bl", width_hint = 0.5) +     annotation_north_arrow(location = "bl", which_north = "true",          pad_x = unit(0.75, "in"), pad_y = unit(0.5, "in"),         style = north_arrow_fancy_orienteering) +     coord_sf(xlim = c(-102.15, -74.12), ylim = c(7.65, 33.97)) #> Scale on map varies by more than 10%, scale bar may be inaccurate annotation_scale(location = "bl", width_hint = 0.5) + annotation_north_arrow(   location = "bl",   which_north = "true",   pad_x = unit(0.75, "in"),   pad_y = unit(0.5, "in"),   style = north_arrow_fancy_orienteering ) coord_sf(   xlim = c(-102.15, -74.12),   ylim = c(7.65, 33.97) )

title: Lecture 3: ggplot2
text: Let´s load required packages: ISO3C code appended HDI_data2 dataset based country name. (note create new dataset HDI_data2) Next, world hdi_country2 datasets merged using left_join() function: Let´s make new map:  Remove Antarctica make new map:  coordinate reference system improved adding coord_sf(crs = “+proj=robin”).  can also add title caption:
code: library(dplyr) library(countrycode) library(spatialcourseOL) data(HDI_data)  head(HDI_data) HDI_data2 <- HDI_data %>%   mutate(iso3 = countrycode(Country, "country.name", "iso3c")) #> Warning: There was 1 warning in `mutate()`. #> ℹ In argument: `iso3 = countrycode(Country, "country.name", "iso3c")`. #> Caused by warning: #> ! Some values were not matched unambiguously: Micronesia #> To fix unmatched values, please use the `custom_match` argument. If you think the default matching rules should be improved, please file an issue at https://github.com/vincentarelbundock/countrycode/issues joined <- world %>%   left_join(     HDI_data2,     by = c("adm0_iso" = "iso3")) #> Warning in sf_column %in% names(g): Detected an unexpected many-to-many relationship between `x` and `y`. #> ℹ Row 193 of `x` matches multiple rows in `y`. #> ℹ Row 192 of `y` matches multiple rows in `x`. #> ℹ If a many-to-many relationship is expected, set `relationship = #>   "many-to-many"` to silence this warning. ggplot() +   geom_sf(     data = joined,     aes(fill = HDI_2023),     color = "grey70",     linewidth = 0.2) +   scale_fill_viridis_c(     option = "plasma",     na.value = "grey85",     name = "HDI (2023)") +   coord_sf(expand = FALSE) +   labs(title = "Human Development Index by countries") +   theme_minimal() joined2<-joined |> dplyr::filter(continent != "Antarctica") ggplot() +   geom_sf(     data = joined2,     aes(fill = HDI_2023),     color = "grey70",     linewidth = 0.2) +   scale_fill_viridis_c(     option = "plasma",     na.value = "grey85",     name = "HDI (2023)") +   coord_sf(expand = FALSE) +   labs(title = "Human Development Index by countries") +   theme_minimal() ggplot() +   geom_sf(     data = joined,     aes(fill = HDI_2023),     color = "grey70",     linewidth = 0.2   ) +   coord_sf(crs = "+proj=eqearth") +   theme_minimal() ggplot() +   geom_sf(     data = joined2,     aes(fill = HDI_2023),     color = "grey70",     linewidth = 0.2   ) +   coord_sf(crs = "+proj=eqearth") +   theme_minimal() + labs(title = "Human Development Index by countries") +   labs(fill = "HDI 2023") +   labs(caption="Digital Geosciences, UEF") +   theme(legend.title = element_text(size=16)) + theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 20))+   theme(plot.caption = element_text(size=16) )

what: Working with ggplot2: Population development of the municipality categories
title: Lecture 3: ggplot2
text: notes explain R script produces faceted bar chart describing population development across different municipality categories Finland. Explanation: data() loads dataset already available R environment loaded package. pop_category contains longitudinal population data, structured : Year Municipality category Population change components (e.g. natural change, migration) dataset long-format foundation visualization. Explanation: code creates named character vector relabeling factor levels. names correspond original category labels data (Finnish). values English translations used plot. vector later passed labeller() improve readability facet titles. Next, draw plot visualising population development Finnish municipality categories. Let’s call plot1 object see plot just created:
code: data(pop_category) group.labs <- c("Sparsely populate", "Urban", "Rural close to urban", "Core rural") names(group.labs) <- c("Harvaan asuttu maaseutu","Kaupungit","Kaupunkien läh. maaseutu", "Ydinmaaseutu") plot1<-ggplot(pop_category, aes(Year, weight=value, fill=Variable))+    geom_bar(binwidth=1, color="gray", size=0.25) +    facet_wrap(~Category, scales = "free_y", ncol = 2, labeller = labeller(Category=group.labs)) +   theme_minimal() +   scale_fill_tableau("Tableau 20", labels=c("Natural population change","Net immigration","Net migration")) +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") + guides(col=guide_legend(ncol=2))+   theme(axis.text.x = element_text(color = "grey20", size = 10, angle = 90, hjust = .5, vjust = .5, face = "plain"),         axis.text.y = element_text(color = "grey20", size = 12, angle = 0, hjust = 1, vjust = 0, face = "plain"),           axis.title.x = element_text(color = "grey20", size = 12, angle = 0, hjust = .5, vjust = 0, face = "plain"),         axis.title.y = element_text(color = "grey20", size = 12, angle = 90, hjust = .5, vjust = .5, face = "plain"),         strip.text = element_text(size = 14)) +   theme(legend.text=element_text(size=14)) +   labs(fill="Population development")+   labs(title="Population development in municipality categories",         x="Year", y="Change, person",        caption="Data: Statistics Finland 2023") +   scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1993,2022,1))+   theme(legend.title = element_text(size=14)) + theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 16)) #> Warning in geom_bar(binwidth = 1, color = "gray", size = 0.25): Ignoring #> unknown parameters: `binwidth` and `size` plot1

what: Adding a simple map to the plot
title: Lecture 3: ggplot2
text: Load packages used example: Let’s load data different regional divisions. Let’s download municipality data set geofi package: Next join data sets function right_join maintains class municipality sf. Let’s define colors plots: set global theme defaults Finally able draw map using ggplot:
code: library(ggplot2) library(geofi) library(dplyr) data(aluejaot2) names(aluejaot2) municipalities <- geofi::get_municipalities(year = 2021) #> Requesting response from: https://geo.stat.fi/geoserver/wfs?service=WFS&version=1.0.0&request=getFeature&typename=tilastointialueet%3Akunta4500k_2021 #> Warning: Coercing CRS to epsg:3067 (ETRS89 / TM35FIN) #>  #> geofi R package: tools for open GIS data for Finland. #> Part of rOpenGov <ropengov.org>. #> Version 1.2.1 #> Data is licensed under: Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0) municipalities <- municipalities %>%    select(kunta, kunta_name) muni <- dplyr::right_join(x = municipalities, y = aluejaot2, by=c("kunta" = "tunnus")) red<-"#F8766D" green <- "#00BA38" blue <- "#619CFF" purple <- "#C77CFF" blue_gray <- "#464a62" mid_gray <- "#ccd0dd" light_gray <- "#f9f9fd" theme_set(theme_minimal()) theme_update(text = element_text(family = "sans", color = "#464a62")) theme_update(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold")) theme_update(plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5)) map<-ggplot(muni) +   geom_sf(aes(fill = Alueluokka_eng), color = light_gray, lwd = 0.08) +   scale_fill_manual(values = c(red, green, blue, purple), name = "", guide = guide_legend(direction = "horizontal", label.position = "top", keywidth = 3, keyheight = 0.5)) +   labs(title = "", color="black") +   theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(),         panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),         axis.text = element_blank(),         axis.title = element_blank(),         legend.position = c(0.25,0.60))+   guides(fill=guide_legend(nrow=4, byrow=T)) +   theme(legend.text=element_text(size=11)) +   theme(plot.margin=margin(0,0,0,0,"cm"))

title: Lecture 3: ggplot2
text: Faceting means splitting one plot multiple small plots, showing subset data, : using variables geoms aesthetics  code, explained line line: initializes plot vs data frame Group.1 - x-axis (likely year) x - y-axis (population similar variable) Draws line panel geom_line() connects points order x linewidth = 1 makes line thicker readable Rotates x-axis labels angle = 90 → vertical labels vjust, hjust → alignment adjustments common x-axis labels long many (e.g. years). key faceting line tells ggplot: “Create one panel unique value Group.2.” Group.2 contains municipalities, get: one panel per municipality contains regions categories: one panel per region/category Internally, ggplot: Splits data Group.2 Draws plot subset Arranges panels automatically rows columns scales = “free_y” — important: facet gets y-axis scale Makes small large groups equally visible Improves readability comparison shape (trends) Basically, code draws separate line plot value Group.2, arranged automatically page, plot showing x changes Group.1 using y-axis scale.
code: ggplot(vs, aes(x=Group.1, y=x))+   geom_line(linewidth=1) +   theme(axis.text.x=element_text(angle=90, vjust=0.5,hjust=1)) +   facet_wrap(facets = ~Group.2, scales = "free_y") ggplot(vs, aes(x = Group.1, y = x)) + geom_line(linewidth = 1) + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1)) + facet_wrap(facets = ~Group.2, scales = "free_y")

title: Lecture 4: Regression analysis
text: Let’s assume want model response Y terms three predictors; X1, X2 X3. One general form model : multiple linear regression model can written Y=β0+β1X1+β2X2+β3X3+ε Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \beta_3 X_3 + \varepsilon - YY dependent variable - β0\beta_0 intercept - β1,β2,β3\beta_1, \beta_2, \beta_3 regression coefficients - X1,X2,X3X_1, X_2, X_3 explanatory variables - ε\varepsilon error term

title: Lecture 4: Regression analysis
text: regression coefficients β0,β1,β2,β3\beta_0, \beta_1, \beta_2, \beta_3 usually estimated using ordinary least squares (OLS). idea OLS choose coefficient values minimize sum squared residuals: ∑=1n(Yi−Ŷ)2 \sum_{=1}^{n} (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2 Ŷ\hat{Y}_i predicted value YY observation ii. standard assumptions, OLS estimators unbiased efficient.

previous_headings: Regression analysis > Estimation of the model
title: Lecture 4: Regression analysis
text: regression coefficient clear interpretation: β0\beta_0 (intercept): expected value YY predictors equal zero. β1\beta_1: expected change YY one‑unit increase X1X_1, holding X2X_2 X3X_3 constant. β2,β3\beta_2, \beta_3: Interpreted analogously X2X_2 X3X_3. “else equal” interpretation central multiple regression analysis.

title: Lecture 4: Regression analysis
text: linear regression model valid, following assumptions commonly made: Linearity relationship predictors response linear. Independence Observations independent . Homoscedasticity variance error term ε\varepsilon constant across observations. Normality errors error term ε\varepsilon normally distributed (mainly important inference). perfect multicollinearity Predictors perfectly correlated . Violations assumptions can lead biased estimates invalid inference.

title: Lecture 4: Regression analysis
text: Two commonly used measures : R2R^2 proportion variance YY explained model. Adjusted R2R^2 Adjusts R2R^2 number predictors preferred comparing models. coefficient determination defined R2=1−Residual Sum SquaresTotal Sum Squares R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{Residual Sum Squares}}{\text{Total Sum Squares}}

title: Lecture 4: Regression analysis
text: Possible extensions basic linear regression framework include: interaction effects (e.g. X1×X2X_1 \times X_2) nonlinear terms (e.g. polynomials) generalized linear models spatial regression models Look webpage https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/r-language/regression-analysis--r-programming/

title: Lecture 4: Regression analysis
text: Regression diagnostics used evaluate whether assumptions linear regression model reasonably satisfied identify potentially influential observations. section, illustrate regression diagnostics using example dataset.

what: Diagnostics for a multiple linear regression model
title: Lecture 4: Regression analysis
text: use savings dataset faraway package. First, fit multiple linear regression model inspect standard diagnostic plots. R automatically produces four standard diagnostic plots linear regression model using plot() function. plots help assess model assumptions identify problematic observations.  Residuals vs. Fitted values plot examines whether relationship predictors response approximately linear. Interpretation: desirable see residuals evenly scattered around horizontal line zero. Systematic patterns (curvature funnel shapes) may indicate nonlinearity heteroscedasticity. Normal Q–Q plot plot assesses whether residuals approximately normally distributed. Interpretation: residuals follow dashed reference line closely, normality assumption reasonable. Strong deviations line suggest departures normality. Scale–Location plot Also called Spread–Location plot, diagnostic evaluates assumption constant variance (homoscedasticity). Interpretation: roughly horizontal line evenly spread points desirable. Increasing decreasing spread suggests heteroscedasticity. Residuals vs. Leverage plot identifies observations may disproportionate influence fitted model. Interpretation: Observations high leverage large residuals potentially influential. Points lying outside Cook’s distance contours may substantially affect model estimates. Summary Diagnostic plots provide essential insight : linearity normality residuals constant variance influence individual observations serious violations detected, remedial measures transforming variables, adding nonlinear terms, using alternative models considered.
code: library(faraway)  data(savings) ?savings  g <- lm(sr ~ pop15 + pop75 + dpi + ddpi, data = savings) par(mfrow = c(2, 2)) plot(g)

title: Lecture 4: Regression analysis
text: diagnostics matter logistic regression? Model diagnostics help answer four key questions: model fit data reasonably well? predictors behaving expected? influential problematic observations? model assumptions seriously violated? Logistic regression assume normal errors constant variance, rely important assumptions. 1. Overall model fit Residual deviance vs. null deviance summary(model1): Key quantities: Null deviance: model predictors Residual deviance: model predictors Rule thumb Residual deviance clearly smaller null deviance. similar, predictors add little explanatory power. Likelihood ratio test can formally test whether full model better null model: small p‑value indicates model improves fit relative intercept‑model. 2. Goodness fit Pseudo‑R² Logistic regression ordinary R², pseudo‑R² measures useful: Common interpretation: 0.02–0.05: weak fit 0.10–0.20: moderate fit 0.20: strong fit (social sciences) directly comparable linear regression R². Classification performance (optional) outcome kasvu binary (0/1): helps evaluate prediction accuracy, replace statistical diagnostics. 3. Residual diagnostics Deviance residuals Plot residuals fitted values:  look : Residuals roughly centered around zero strong patterns funnels Problems: Curvature - missing nonlinear terms Extreme vertical spreads - outliers poor fit Residuals vs. predictors Example:  Residuals show systematic trends. 4. Influential observations data points may disproportionate influence results. () Cook’s distance  Observations line deserve inspection. Leverage influence  High leverage points automatically wrong, understood substantively. 5. Multicollinearity diagnostics Highly correlated predictors can inflate standard errors. Rule thumb: VIF < 5 - usually acceptable VIF > 10 - serious multicollinearity 6. Functional form (linearity logit) Logistic regression assumes continuous predictors linearly related log‑odds, outcome . Visual check (example) Curvature suggests may need: transformations, polynomial terms, splines.

text: variogram describes similar (dissimilar) values spatial variable function distance locations. simple terms: Nearby locations tend similar values distant locations, variogram quantifies relationship. variogram shows pairs observations distance hhh, variogram measures average squared difference: γ(h)=12𝔼[(Z(s)−Z(s+h))2] \gamma(h) = \frac{1}{2}\,\mathbb{E}\left[(Z(s) - Z(s+h))^2\right] Small distances → small differences (high similarity) Large distances → large differences (low similarity) Key concepts Nugget: variability small distances (measurement error microscale variation) Sill: total spatial variance Range: distance beyond locations longer spatially correlated Graphically: curve rises distance, levels sill. variograms matter variogram essential kriging : models spatial autocorrelation, determines observations weighted prediction, controls predictions uncertainty. variogram - kriging. Sample variogram apply geostatistical interpolation, first model spatial dependence using variogram.  default, variogram function: sets cutoff one-third bounding box diagonal, divides 15 distance bins. code: mod.lo <- loess(prestige ~ income + education, span=.5, degree=1) summary(mod.lo) ## Call: ## loess(formula = prestige ~ income + education, span = 0.5, degree = 1) ##  ## Number of Observations: 102  ## Equivalent Number of Parameters: 8.03  ## Residual Standard Error: 6.906  ## Trace of smoother matrix: 10.5  (exact) ##  ## Control settings: ##   span     :  0.5  ##   degree   :  1  ##   family   :  gaussian ##   surface  :  interpolate      cell = 0.2 ##   normalize:  TRUE ##  parametric:  FALSE FALSE ## drop.square:  FALSE FALSE inc <- seq(min(income), max(income), len=25) ed <- seq(min(education), max(education), len=25) newdata <- expand.grid(income=inc,education=ed) fit.prestige <- matrix(predict(mod.lo, newdata), 25, 25) persp(inc, ed, fit.prestige, theta=45, phi=30,  xlab="Income", ylab="Education", zlab="Prestige", expand=2/3, shade=0.5)

title: Lecture 5: Nonparametric regression
text: Smoothing splines arise solution following simple regression problem: find function f̂\hat f minimizes penalized sum squares: SS(h)=∑=1n[yi−f(xi)]2+h∫xminxmax[f″(x)]2dx \mathrm{SS}(h) = \sum_{=1}^{n} \bigl[y_i - f(x_i)\bigr]^2 + h \int_{x_{\min}}^{x_{\max}} \bigl[f''(x)\bigr]^2 \, dx h smoothing parameter, analogous neighborhood-width local-polynomial estimator. Error z value Pr(>|z|)     ## (Intercept)  -1.0238     0.1652  -6.196 5.79e-10 *** ## --- ## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 ##  ## Approximate significance of smooth terms: ##             edf Ref.df Chi.sq  p-value     ## s(DIS456) 8.593  8.915 54.949  < 2e-16 *** ## s(DIS20)  5.107  6.210 41.678 2.83e-06 *** ## s(DIS10)  1.000  1.000  0.432  0.51082     ## s(DIS6)   6.156  7.280 19.522  0.00872 **  ## --- ## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 ##  ## R-sq.(adj) =  0.324   Deviance explained = 29.1% ## UBRE = -0.0072223  Scale est. = 1         n = 460 plot(y_gam,pages=1,ylim=c(-5,5)) ccont=ctree_control(maxdepth=3) fit2=ctree(y_gam$fit~x$DIS456+x$DIS20+x$DIS6,control=ccont) plot(fit2)
